item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. You have a notebook problem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I'll be the first to admit that since being a member here for almost 10 years I haven't read every SCP. There's a lot. Sometimes I hit "Random SCP" and get a new one. I'm probably worst with Series 1 SCPs.

When I joined the SCP wiki in 2011/12ish, it was a rediscovery. See, I had briefly posted SCPs when they were first originating on /x/. I was really happy to see how those fun threads had turned into a whole, functioning community. I never gave any thought to the idea that anything I'd posted on /x/ had survived.

Until I ran across this article.

For you all that know me, I'm an author. Like a real-life author of books. I've been a writer my whole life. And as a writer, I have a…shall we say…notebook problem.

That top notebook is special: that's my "Yellow File". It originally started as a notebook to keep NPCs for as storyteller of my local Vampire:The Masquerade LARP. See here? That's me vainly putting my name at the top. After a while it became my notebook where I'd transfer what I thought were my really good ideas (for example, the first "official" notes of my 2016 tabletop RPG Cold Start are in this notebook). I still use it today.

Of course, back when I used it for Vampire, it was just a notebook for whatever. I brought it with me everywhere. (Yes, I was that kid, wearing all black, with long hair and a trenchcoat, scribbling in a notebook.) And if I had a cool idea I'd scribble it in there.

Which is why, stuck between two way-too-powerful Vampire NPCs, I found this.

As you can see, 017 went through some changes between /x/ and the Wiki. I never actually joined the wiki myself, because I was out of the loop when it started, and had drifted onto other things when it happened. But I think it is really awesome that SCP-017 has survived, and to stumble across it now, over a decade later, is nothing short of fucking amazing. I love this community. Thanks for making it what it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So beautifully simple… I absolutely love this one

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: short and sweet. nice

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image could not be sourced reliably, the earliest non-SCP usage of the image originating from here, which I suggest not clicking. As such, the image is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0 and is pending staff evaluation for removal/replacement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not to mention that the box test won't work in all cases for all skips; it's always going to be subject to interpretation, variance, and sometimes straight-up won't apply.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Old skips go by old standards.

If I'm not mistaken, this predates the invention of the box test as a concept.
This could be taken as both an irl explanation and an in-universe justification.

Read this as an older file in the archives. It met the standards for keter when written, and The Foundation revised the official definition of the term between then and now. The report is slated for review and updating, but that fix hasn't happened yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know people have been explaining why is keter, but it still doesn't make sense. According to the box test, this should be euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The containment procedures are fairly complex, with little margin for error.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is it keter?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1, simple but interesting. Also I can’t help but think of Mimikyu

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: -1 for sure. When you make something as mundane as a cloud of smoke into an scp I really think you have to provide a substantial amount of back story to really make it interesting. How does it react to other forms of light (UV, Infrared, etc)? How about transparent items? Any mention of them at least trying to figure out what happens to the things it consumes would also help a lot. Super hard to believe that a group of scientists would just say "it eats things and makes them go away" and that's that. Perhaps the addition of the document is supposed to make us assume that there's more info backing this than we're allowed to see but, frankly, that's just a cop out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For future reference:

http://www.scp-wiki.net/experiment-log-t-98816-oc108-682

Second entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
STAND NAME: BLACK SABBATH
STAND MASTER: POLPO

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The general rule about 682 (which I assume is who you meant) is that no matter what you throw at it, it never dies. This forces anyone who writes an encounter/cross-test between the hard-to-kill reptile and another SCP to be creative in describing how the lizard would counteract the anomalous effects. I imagine 682 might burst out of 017 like a chick from an egg, but that's just one possible scenario.

EDIT: On an unrelated foray into 682's experiment log, I discovered that this has already been attempted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So does SCP-017 attack things that cast shadows on it, because it wants to attack evetything and can't until it has a line of shadow to move through…
Or because casting a shadow on it somehow aggrivates it, and it feels the need to destroy the source of its anguish?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know this is a really old SCP but… a question: if SCP 618- the reptile- cast a shadow on this kid would it die??? would it disappear???? Foundation researchers I demand an explanation.
As the 500th person to upvote this, I wonder why I never did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are a couple works that expand upon this listed in the Series 1 Tale List.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the concept and execution of this SCP, but I find myself wanting more. Maybe more in depth information, a history, or an incident report of some kind. I'm sure that won't happen given how old this one is, but it really piques my interest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-1, not that it'll do much.
I'm firmly of the belief that just because something's old doesn't mean it's untouchable. Hell, this doesn't even have the excuse that SCPs like 173 have of being grandfathered in by crosslinks and stories and culture. I didn't even know about this one until now.

When it comes down to it, this is just a thing-what-eats-you and nothing else, and the article is 2/3 containment procedures. Sure, apparently there's a Serpent's Hand page that makes this sort of interesting, but an article shouldn't need a separate, un-linked page to be interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Welcome! Whatever you do, don't go around making those kind of changes to articles. Only the author can do that; you can suggest it in the discussion page, just like you did here, but for the most part you can assume that a posted article (especially one that's been around for a while and is rated highly) is finished as-is, imperfections and all. Anyone can make minor edits to an article, like fixing obvious spelling errors or adding a rate module if the author forgot one; nobody, staff or otherwise, is allowed to change the meaning of the article itself.

Lots of articles refer to supplementary documents or appendices that don't exist - that's usually the author purposefully leaving something to your imagination. If there's some kind of experiment you'd like to see this in, you're free to write about it yourself and post it as a tale separate from this article. You can get more help with this process in the ideas and brainstorming and draft forums.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, since I'm new here, I want to understand something: is it dumb to ask to someone to make the document #017-1? Or to delete the paragraph that mentions it? Could I myself edit it or, since I'm a huge noob here still, just hope that someone more acquainted would care enough to do it?

I know this may kinda be read in a sarcastic way, but I don't it mean it this way, I'm honestly lost about this (this is why I'll hardly risk editing any SCP, the chances of misunderstanding something or being misunderstood are too high for me)

item.title: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded:
For those who'd like to listen instead of read, I hope you enjoy this reading:

https://youtu.be/dYFHKvali3g

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Although the article is trying to give me the "oooh, spooky scare" vibe. I will give it the benefit of the doubt because it is an early series 1 SCP.

I will give it a +1, because this was a step in the dark for defining Keter classes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It literally says right there in the staff notice that it was because he was trolling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Staff notice: This user has been permanently banned for trolling.

item.title: Reminds me of something
item.content:encoded: This SCP reminds me of the humanity sprites in Dark Souls!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Staff notice: this user is permanently banned for trolling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Apologies for necro, but it seems that light in general makes it smaller, perhaps burning away bits and pieces of it. Depending on the time period it was captured, spotlights can easily take it down or even camera flashes, depending on how fast it can spread in darkness. What didn't allow it to basically kill make everyone disappear off the planet before being found is most likely the Sun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just don't thrust your master sword towards it; it'll just jump on it.

item.title: Re: Suggested use.
item.content:encoded: They did already.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How did they contain this thing in the first place?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would have liked it if there was a little more story to it, but considering it has over 300 rating, it's probably good enough already.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is it the shadow that makes SCP-17 aggressive, or does it just eat anything that comes near that isn't luminescent?

Answer: I don't know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think there is a size issue, and the Foundation is probably making it pissed so it's taking every opportunity to hurt its captors. In personal opinion, if the Foundation was not blasting it from every angle with the thing it apparently hates, it probably wouldn't be Keter.

So, no, It wouldn't eat the whole site if the lights went out.

item.title: SCP-435?
item.content:encoded: "He-Who-Makes-Dark"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: People probably like the simplicity of it and the picture. There's also some hints of it being something greater, like some ancient deity or whatever, in Serpent's Hand documents and such.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So it's a shadow that eats you if you cast a shadow on it?

Am I missing something here? The rating is so high I feel like I must be.

item.title: Re: Suggested use.
item.content:encoded:
Upon repeatedly being introduced to SCP-017, SCP-682 advances to Destructive Archangel class, radiates blinding light beams that can vaporize water at close-range, and SCP-017 is never seen again.

The resultant mess takes the Foundation six months and twelve months of backup funding to fix.

item.title: Re: Suggested use.
item.content:encoded: Plus, in the SCP-682 experiment termination logs, the Foundation DOES introduce it to SCP-682 (it's the second one on the page), but it doesn't end up working the way they had hoped.

item.title: Re: Suggested use.
item.content:encoded:
Because in universe, the Foundation practices safety and strives to better contain SCPs. They are not geared for making SCPs interact with one another, and cause unpredictable and potentially dangerous occurrences.

While we (the out-of-universe readers) may be curious about it, let us ask ourselves: Does it make sense in-universe? If you're a Foundation bureaucrat making the same decision (with the same stakes as the Foundation would have), would you allow or veto it?

item.title: Suggested use.
item.content:encoded: I don't see why those Foundation idiots don't put it in with 682. LOL.

item.title: Positive Review
item.content:encoded: I find this SCP particularly awesome, as it's frightening concept (seriously, empty shadowy shroud that engulfs you! Aaargh!) is coupled with a minimalistic presentation that leaves much to the imagination. It's as brief and consise as it needs to be, and I love the containment procedures; particularly the high-reflective clothing, very well thought out.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Noting that a recent edit introducing a link to a tale (the author of said tale has commented that it "definitely shouldn't be" linked) has been reverted, twice.

Staff consensus is that it is always up to the original author to decide if they want any crosslinks on their article. Please obtain explicit permission from the author if you wish to make any such changes; if the author is inactive, contact a staff member and ask for their input.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not the hugest fan of the "less you know" types of SCP, I generally prefer the ones with lots of info to mull over and allow me to creep myself out over it. This just gives far too little. I know what it is vaguely and how it's contained which is fine but nothing here is all that interesting. If the addendum document existed then I'd probably have stronger feelings about this. As it is, downvoted due to lack of substance.

EDIT: Didn't mean this to be a reply to the above post… not sure how I did that. Regardless, post stands although it wasn't meant as a reply to Mistbourne.

item.title: Re: if I May
item.content:encoded: It doesn't shut off the sun, but that means only anything between it and sun will be consumed. So it would make a huge hole from its containment to surface? Perhaps it needs to be able to completely surround the object before it can consume it. So if it is in a completely sealed box with no way out it would not be able to consume the box around it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sometimes, less is more. I don't feel that this is the case with this article.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I also enjoy this one. I definitely think it seems a bit lacking, though the linked Serpent's Hand document above covers everything I'd wanna see in this and makes it more fleshed out and wonderful anyway. It was also one of the first I read (when I wasn't even reading in numerical order) and probably the first to legitimately scare the crap out of me. So yeah, in some regards, I like this a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahhhhh fuckin' love this SCP! I know others have differing opinions about it, but this is the 2nd article I ever read and it holds a special place in my heart. <3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Poorly stated

This is unnecessary. If something's confusing to you, ask for clarification. If you just feel that it could have been stated better, that doesn't mean it's automatically "poorly" stated.

And of course, keep in mind that this is a rather old article, written years before today's standards were established.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay so it isn't my favourite post or anything, but that aside: arc spotlights are nearly 125 years old. They suck, they flicker, albeit they're bright. They also actually have bulbs. Which doesn't have to be the case? If permissed it would be a simple change to make it LED or flourescent. Something that, indefinitely, doesn't need replaced. Just saying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Poorly stated, but I don't see the benefit of this either. Its abilities come off rather unclear, and it isn't too interesting a concept. It's also debatably not Keter, but that's arguable

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agent Edvin, you're replying to a post almost three years old now.

Also, 173 is perhaps the oldest article on the site, and thus is not considered exemplary of today's standards. Its high rating is attributed mainly to its historical significance to the site, being the "first" SCP article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i realize that, i did not see the document on the bottom of 682s page before,

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Already been done, on the 682 experiment log. Didn't work.

Also, keep in mind that 1. roleplaying is not allowed on the forums, so make sure your posts aren't in-character and 2. 682 cannot be destroyed. That's the narrative hook there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
could destruction of SCP-682 be possible if introduced with SCP-017 ?

the procedure to do so would have to be complex but if it works then SCP-682 would be destroyed,

the risks do seem to outway the benefit in this case though, but it is still a possibility,

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A direct link to Document #017-1 would get me to upvote this. As it is, the lack of details shows the article's age.

No vote.
